---
title: "Hyejung Lee"
---
<!-- about: -->
<!--   template: solana -->
<!--   #image: img/headshot.jpg -->
<!--   links: -->
<!--     - icon: github -->
<!--       text: GitHub -->
<!--       href: https://github.com/hyejung0 -->
<!--     - icon: linkedin -->
<!--       text: LinkedIn -->
<!--       href: https://www.linkedin.com/in/hyejung-lee-b2249819b/ -->
<!--     - icon: envelope -->
<!--       text: E-Mail -->
<!--       href: mailto:hyejung.lee@utah.edu -->
<!--     - icon: file-earmark-arrow-down -->
<!--       text: CV -->
<!--       href: CV.pdf -->



# About Me

I’m a PhD biostatistician based in Utah. What I care about most is doing research that helps real people in a real way—especially people and communities that are underserved.

I’ve always loved the “why” behind things. That is part of what pulled me into math first, and then into statistics. But over time, what kept me here was not just the methods themselves. It was realizing that good statistical work can shape real decisions: what gets studied, how care is delivered, where resources go, and whether research actually reaches the people who need it most.

My training is in biostatistics and population health, and my work so far has touched several areas of medicine and health research. I do enjoy the technical side of things—causal inference, survival analysis, Bayesian modeling, and building tools in R. But honestly, I’m not attached to doing something flashy just because it sounds impressive. I don’t mind doing simple statistics at all if the work has direct impact on people’s lives.

These days, I’m especially interested in collaborative, mission-driven research. The kind where the question actually matters, the results are actionable, and the work has a real chance of improving care, access, or outcomes for underserved populations locally, and globally.

Long term, I want my career to reflect both what I’m good at and what I care about. For me, statistics is not just a technical skill. It’s a way to be useful.

Outside of work, I stay connected with family abroad, catch up with friends, go to church on Sundays, and watch Korean shows. Okay, that sounds like a pretty good life now that I write it out.


# Research Interests

- **Precision Medicine**  
  This is an umbrella term that encompasses any research aimed at tailoring treatments to subgroups or individuals who share common characteristics, with the goal of helping each person receive the most optimal treatment.
- **Causal Inference**
  Rather than the traditional associational study, I like doing things that can be more like "causal" in nature! 
- **Dynamic Treatment Regimes**  
  Identifying sequence of treatment choices that yields the best outcome of interest
- **Bayesian Nonparametric**  
  Just started learning about this field. I like the method is more truly following the Bayesian philosophy, rather 
    
  
  
  
# Education

**University of Utah** | Salt Lake City, UT, U.S.A                                         
PhD in Population Health Sciences, Division of Biostatistics | 2020 - 2025


**Brigham Young University** | Provo, UT, U.S.A                                               
M.S. in Statistics | 2018 - 2020


**University of Calgary** | Calgary, AB, Canada                                      
B.Sc. in Pure Mathematics with minor in Applied Mathematics | 2010 -2014



# Experience


**Division of Epidemiology, Department of Internal Medicine, University of Utah** | Research Associate | 2025 - present

**Huntsman Cancer Institute** | Graduate Research Assistant | Aug 2020 - 2025

**Biostatistics Intern** | Regeneron Pharmaceuticals | May 2024 - Aug 2024

**Brigham Young University** | Graduate Research Assistant | Aug 2018 - Aug 2020




## Contact

- **Email:** [hyejung.lee@utah.edu](mailto:hyejung.lee@utah.edu)
- **GitHub:** [hyejung0](https://github.com/hyejung0)
- **LinkedIn:** [Profile](https://www.linkedin.com/in/hyejung-lee-b2249819b/)
- **CV:** [Download PDF](CV.pdf)
